abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) is a central neuromodulator which is implicated in, amongst other functions, cognitive flexibility. 5-HT is released from the dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) throughout nearly the entire forebrain. Little is known, however, about how serotonin affects downstream populations of neurons and how this modulation might support its cognitive functions. Here, we optogenetically stimulated serotonergic neurons in the DRN while recording large parts of the brain with Neuropixels during quiet wakefulness and performance of a perceptual decision-making task. During quiet wakefulness, 5-HT stimulation induced a rapid switch in internal state, as indicated by dilation of the pupil, suppression of hippocampal sharp wave ripples, and increased exploratory behaviors, such as whisking. To elucidate the brain-wide effect of optogenetic activation of serotonergic neurons on single neuron spiking activity, we performed acute Neuropixels recordings across seven target trajectories, recording 7,478 neurons across 17 mice. We found that 5-HT stimulation significantly modulated neural activity in all recorded brain regions, both during quiet wakefulness and task performance. During task performance, however, activation of serotonergic neurons had no effect on behavior. Using a generalized linear model, we show that serotonergic modulation combines additively with choice-related activity, as opposed to a multiplicative gain modulation; the choice-by-stimulation interaction was near zero across regions and animals. As a geometric consequence of this additivity, 5-HT modulation was confined to a subspace orthogonal to the choice axis. These findings suggest that serotonin can reconfigure distributed neural dynamics without interfering with ongoing task computations.

sentences.text: Serotonin (5-HT) is a central neuromodulator which is implicated in, amongst other functions, cognitive flexibility.
sentences.note: General serotonin / cognitive-flexibility framing; sets up the question but is not itself a claim of this paper. Identical to v1 sentence 1.

sentences.text: 5-HT is released from the dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) throughout nearly the entire forebrain.
sentences.note: Anatomical background motivating the brain-wide recording strategy. Identical to v1 sentence 2.

sentences.text: Little is known, however, about how serotonin affects downstream populations of neurons and how this modulation might support its cognitive functions.
sentences.note: Gap statement; motivates the study. Identical to v1 sentence 3.

sentences.text: Here, we optogenetically stimulated serotonergic neurons in the DRN while recording large parts of the brain with Neuropixels during quiet wakefulness and performance of a perceptual decision-making task.
sentences.note: Methods/scope sentence — names the SERT-Cre + ChR2 protocol and the brain-wide Neuropixels strategy. Identical to v1 sentence 4.

sentences.text: During quiet wakefulness, 5-HT stimulation induced a rapid switch in internal state, as indicated by dilation of the pupil, suppression of hippocampal sharp wave ripples, and increased exploratory behaviors, such as whisking.
sentences.note: State-switch interpretive claim plus its three converging empirical readouts (pupil, SWR, exploratory behavior) all bundled into one sentence. Identical to v1 sentence 5.

sentences.text: To elucidate the brain-wide effect of optogenetic activation of serotonergic neurons on single neuron spiking activity, we performed acute Neuropixels recordings across seven target trajectories, recording 7,478 neurons across 17 mice.
sentences.note: CHANGED FROM v1: 'strong synchronous activation' is replaced with 'optogenetic activation' to avoid overstating optogenetic uniformity (Dugué 2014, ChR2 adaptation, light fall-off). The dataset numbers are identical. The framing change is significant: it scopes the result to what optogenetics achieves rather than asserting strong synchronous physiological activation.

sentences.text: We found that 5-HT stimulation significantly modulated neural activity in all recorded brain regions, both during quiet wakefulness and task performance.
sentences.note: RESTORED IN R1 AUTHORITATIVE — this sentence was missing from the previous (revision-plan-based) draft of the R1 abstract mapping but is present in the actual R1 manuscript. It bundles the brain-wide modulation finding (fig 2) with the cross-condition state-dependent magnitude finding (fig 5d / Fig 5d).

sentences.text: During task performance, however, activation of serotonergic neurons had no effect on behavior.
sentences.note: RESTORED IN R1 AUTHORITATIVE — also missing from the previous draft. The behavioral null is the load-bearing puzzle that motivates the additivity/orthogonality reconciliation.

sentences.text: Using a generalized linear model, we show that serotonergic modulation combines additively with choice-related activity, as opposed to a multiplicative gain modulation; the choice-by-stimulation interaction was near zero across regions and animals.
sentences.note: NEW IN R1 — the central reframing claim. The actual R1 abstract bundles the additivity finding and the gain-control elimination into a single sentence using 'as opposed to a multiplicative gain modulation' rather than (as in the prior draft) splitting them across two sentences. The sentence maps to the central R1 hypothesis (hypothesis-additive-modulation), the central R1 empirical claim (near-zero-choice-by-stim-interaction), its precondition (glm-significant-choice-and-5ht-coefficients), and the eliminative synthesis (rules-out-multiplicative-gain-control). v1 had no GLM analysis at all and no additivity claim in the abstract.

sentences.text: As a geometric consequence of this additivity, 5-HT modulation was confined to a subspace orthogonal to the choice axis.
sentences.note: REFRAMED FROM v1 — orthogonality is now explicitly demoted to a 'geometric consequence' of additivity rather than the central finding. v1 sentence 9 stated orthogonality as the headline; R1 sentence 10 frames it as a derived property. Maps to the new synthesis claim (orthogonality-derived-from-additivity) which expresses the demotion structurally, plus the empirical orthogonality claim and the orthogonal-subspace hypothesis (which itself is now derived-from additivity).

sentences.text: These findings suggest that serotonin can reconfigure distributed neural dynamics without interfering with ongoing task computations.
sentences.note: CHANGED FROM v1 — the v1 closing sentence ('These observations describe a possible mechanism for the induction of state-dependent stimulus representations, suggesting a neural basis for neuromodulatory effects on brain-wide circuits to flexible decision-making') is replaced with this more neutral framing about reconfiguring without interfering. The R1 framing is more honest: it does not claim a mechanism for state-dependent representations (which the data do not directly support), only the integrative observation that 5-HT modulates without disrupting. Maps to the central additivity hypothesis and to the load-bearing behavioral null.
